user_request: Draft a suit for partition and separate possession of ancestral property. Plaintiff entitled to 1/3rd share. Defendant refusing amicable partition. Include genealogy table and schedule of property. Seek preliminary decree. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks partition and separate possession of ancestral property, claiming a 1/3rd share. Defendant is refusing amicable partition, necessitating legal action for a preliminary decree.

Valuation of the property for court fees

6. Partition decree. In a suit for partition, there are normally two decrees: (i) a preliminary decree; and (ii) a final 
decree. There might be a preliminary decree or even more than one preliminary decree, which would determine the 
share of the party, but which would not divide the properties deliverable to the co-sharer. Another stage is that of 
passing of final decree after inquiry is held, as per the directions contained in the preliminary decree. The shares of 
the party are determined first at the stage of preliminary decree, and the actual partition is effected as per the final 
decree.31
When the parties to a suit filed a compromise petition with the plan partitioning their respective shares by metes and 
bounds, no further inquiry is needed unless it is found to be not genuine. In such a case passing a preliminary 
decree is not necessary and the court can directly pass a final decree in terms of the compromise.32
Generally speaking, a final decree cannot amend or go behind the preliminary decree on a matter decided by the 
preliminary decree. 33 However, where some of the parties after the passing of the preliminary decree give up 
certain rights in favour of one of the parties, the effect of such giving up should be given while passing the final

question has sometimes arisen for decision whether there can be more than one preliminary decree and one 
final decree in a suit. On that, judicial opinion was divided; some decisions took the view that there could be 
only one preliminary decree and one final decree,76 while others held that there was nothing in the Code of Civil 
Procedure prohibiting the passing of more than one preliminary or final decree.77
In accordance with this view, where the plaintiff was found to be entitled to contribution but the amount was left 
to be determined later on, it was held that the order was a preliminary decree.78

18. Decree in suits for partition of property or separate possession of a share therein. Where the Court passes a 
decree for the partition of property or for the separate possession of a share therein, the Court may, if the partition or 
separation cannot be conveniently made without further inquiry, pass a preliminary decree declaring the rights of the 
several parties interested in the property and giving such further directions as may be required.
1. Sub-rule (1): Payment of revenue by estate. A decree passed under sub-r (1) is not a preliminary decree. It is 
final, though partition under the decree remains to be effected by the collector. An application filed in court for 
sending the papers to the collector is not an execution application. The act of sending is a ministerial and not a 
judicial function.87
As regards property assessed to payment of revenue to government, after a partition decree declaring shares of the 
parties in the suit property is passed, all further proceedings are required to be taken before the collector or any 
gazetted officer subordinate to him in terms of s 54 and O 20, r 18 of the Code of Civil Procedure. It is not 
necessary for the decree holder to make any application in this regard. It is the duty of the collector as per law.

sections.instruction: Draft the suit title line based on the nature of the suit, relief sought, and any monetary amount involved. Use standard Indian court pleading format. If this is a property suit use 'SUIT FOR PARTITION AND SEPARATE POSSESSION' style. If recovery suit use 'SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/-' style. Adapt to the actual suit type from the classified doc_type and intake facts.

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff(s) and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, address. Use competency averment (sui juris / competent to sue). If any detail is missing, use {{PLACEHOLDER}}. Adapt party labels to the suit type (e.g., Petitioner/Respondent for writ petitions).

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction based on the facts. For territorial jurisdiction, state the SPECIFIC factual basis: where the defendant resides, where property is situated, where the cause of action arose, or where the transaction occurred. For suits involving immovable property, jurisdiction is based on where the property is situated (Section 16 CPC). For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the suit valuation and that it falls within the court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Adapt the narrative to the actual nature of the suit: for property suits describe ownership chain and possession history, for recovery suits describe the transaction and default, for family matters describe relationships and events. Reference documents as Annexure labels. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

must_include.description: At least one date or date placeholder

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section citing relevant statutory provisions and legal principles. For EACH provision: (1) State the legal rule it creates, (2) State what conditions trigger it, (3) Show how the facts satisfy those conditions. Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list or those naturally applicable to the suit type. If applicable, include alternative pleas using 'In the alternative and without prejudice...' framing. Include defensive pleading to pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments.

must_include.description: At least one statutory provision cited

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose. Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose (the specific event: default, refusal, denial, dispossession, etc.), (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — subsequent acts that sustain the right (further demands, further refusals), (3) CONTINUITY — why the cause of action is continuing. Anchor to the actual triggering event from the facts, not to procedural dates like notice.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act 1963 (from enrichment or use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation anchored to the cause of action accrual event, (4) that the suit is within time.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section. State the suit valuation and basis (claim amount for money suits, property value for property suits, etc.). State court fee as per applicable State Court Fees Act or use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder. For property suits, state valuation per Section 7 of the Court Fees Act (market value of share) if applicable.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section IF applicable to this suit type. For money recovery suits: pre-suit interest, pendente lite interest (Section 34 CPC), and future interest with rates and justification. For property suits: mesne profits or compensation if applicable, or state that interest is not the primary relief. Adapt to the actual nature of the suit. If interest is not relevant to this suit type, state briefly that the Plaintiff claims mesne profits / compensation as applicable, or omit interest entirely and instead address any financial relief that fits.

must_include.description: Some form of financial relief or interest stated

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must be specific and tied to the relief sought. Adapt prayers to the actual suit type: for partition suits seek preliminary decree declaring share + final decree for division + separate possession + commissioner appointment; for recovery suits seek decree for principal amount + interest + costs; for injunction suits seek permanent injunction + damages. Always include a general relief clause 'any other relief the Court deems fit'.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon. Map to annexure labels. Adapt documents to the suit type: for property suits include title deeds, genealogy, revenue records, survey sketches; for recovery suits include agreement, payment proof, notice; for family suits include marriage certificate, birth records. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents for this suit type with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, occupation {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, resident at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as the Plaintiff), is sui juris and competent to sue. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, occupation {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, resident at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as the Defendant), is sui juris and competent to be sued. The Plaintiff is the Primary Plaintiff and the Defendant the Opposite Defendant in this partition suit. The parties are co-sharers in the ancestral properties described in Schedule A, which are situate within the jurisdiction of this Court, and the Plaintiff is entitled to one-third share in the said ancestral property by virtue of succession in the Plaintiff's line of descent from the common ancestors. The Defendant refuses amicable partition and denies any settlement of the shares. The genealogy of the parties and the shared ancestors is recorded in a genealogy table annexed as Annexure I. The schedule of properties in which partition is sought is described in Schedule A and annexed as Annexure II (Schedule of Property). The Plaintiff seeks a preliminary decree for partition and separate possession of the one-third share in the ancestral property, and for such other orders as the Court may deem fit.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text: Territorial jurisdiction: This Hon'ble District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}} has territorial jurisdiction to entertain the partition and separate possession suit concerning ancestral immovable property situated at {{PROPERTY_ADDRESS}} within its territorial limits. The governing principle, as per Section 16 CPC, is that suits relating to immovable property must be instituted in the Court having jurisdiction over the place where the property is situated; hence, the situs of the property at {{PROPERTY_ADDRESS}} anchors the territorial jurisdiction of this Court to determine the partition dispute. The present suit seeks a partition of the ancestral property and a preliminary decree for separate possession in respect of the plaintiff's one-third share, and the defendant’s ongoing refusal to amicably partition the property provides the basis for the action arising at the situs of the property. Place of filing and the operative facts underlying the suit render this Court the appropriate forum for adjudication of the partition claim. Pecuniary jurisdiction: The suit valuation is Rs. {{SUIT_VALUATION}}, which is within the monetary limit of this District Court in {{STATE}}, thereby giving this Court the power to adjudicate the partition and related relief sought, including a preliminary decree for partition and possession of the plaintiff’s one-third share. Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}

filled_sections.text:
1. The ancestral property bearing the address {{PROPERTY_ADDRESS}} is traced to the family line from the original ancestor, as shown in the Genealogy Table attached as Annexure A; Annexure A depicts the sequence of inheritance and current co-ownership among the Plaintiff, the Defendant, and other legal heirs, with the property described in Annexure B as located within the jurisdiction of the District Court at {{PLACE_NAME}}.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, seeks partition and separate possession of the ancestral property by filing this suit in the District Court at {{DISTRICT_NAME}} on {{FILING_DATE}}. filled_sections.heading: INTEREST

(e) The Plaintiff prays for any other relief the Court deems fit.

LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Legal Basis and Grounds. Article 109 of the Limitation Act creates a specific limitation rule for a Hindu governed by Mitakshara law to seek to set aside his father’s alienation of ancestral property, prescribing a twelve-year period, with accrual when the alienee takes possession. The triggering condition is the existence of an alienation by the father of ancestral property and the alienee’s possession, within which the aggrieved coparcener must move to challenge the alienation. In the present suit, the Plaintiff seeks partition and separate possession of ancestral property and asserts a 1/3 coparcener share; there is no pleaded challenge to any alienation by the father, and no relief sought to set aside an alienation. Therefore, the triggering condition of Article 109 is not engaged against the relief sought, and the suit is not barred by limitation under this provision. The Plaintiff’s genealogical status as a coparcener and the nature of the property to be partitioned are evidenced by Annexure [GENEALOGY], which corrobates the Plaintiff’s entitlement to a 1/3 share, and Annexure [PROPERTY_SCHEDULE], which identifies the ancestral properties proposed for partition and separate possession. Consequently, the Plaintiff maintains that the claim for partition is timely and within jurisdiction for a preliminary decree to be granted by the District Court at {{DISTRICT_COURT}} on {{FILING_DATE}}, in respect of the property described in Annexure [PROPERTY_SCHEDULE].

In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if any aspect of limitation is raised as a defense, the Plaintiff relies on the general Hindu law principle that a coparcener is entitled to demand partition of ancestral property and possession of his undivided share, independently of any alienation-attack arguments. The Plaintiff’s genealogical evidence demonstrates a 1/3 interest in the ancestral property, and the property schedule identifies the assets subject to partition; Accordingly, the Plaintiff seeks a preliminary decree for partition and separate possession, notwithstanding any potential limitation challenge to other reliefs. This alternative basis is asserted to preserve the Plaintiff’s rights in light of the familial coparcenary framework and to ensure the court can determine shares and possession expeditiously.

Defensive pleading anticipated by Plaintiff. The Defendant is likely to argue, among other points, that the Plaintiff lacks standing as a coparcener or that the property is not ancestral, or that the alleged share is incorrect. The Plaintiff pre-empts these defenses by relying on Annexure [GENEALOGY] to establish lineage and coparcenary status, and Annexure [PROPERTY_SCHEDULE] to confirm the ancestral nature and the scope of the property. The Defendant’s attempts to undermine coparcener status or to contest the ancestral character of the property are inconsistent with established coparcenary principles under Hindu law and are unsupported by the evidentiary Annexures. The Plaintiff therefore prays for issuance of a preliminary decree for partition and separate possession in accordance with the Schedule of Property, pending final partition and conveyed rights, in the District Court at {{DISTRICT_COURT}} on {{FILING_DATE}}.

Article 109 of the Limitation Act 1963 provides that, for a Hindu governed by Mitakshara law, a suit to set aside the father's alienation of ancestral property must be brought within twelve years from the date the alienee takes possession of the ancestral property.
